title: IBHS Wildfire Prepared Goes National: What It Means for California Luxury Homes
subtitle: The certification that shaped California underwriting is now a ten-state standard — raising the bar for what 'insurable' means.
meta_description: IBHS just expanded Wildfire Prepared to 10 more states. Here's what the national rollout means for insurable luxury homes in California and Los Angeles.
meta_keywords: insurable home California, IBHS Wildfire Prepared, fire-resistant home California, luxury home builder Malibu, California fire insurance solution home, wildfire certification

our_perspective: A national certification is a useful forcing function, because it pushes underwriters to price against a single technical checklist rather than local interpretations. But a checklist still measures components — roof class, vent type, Zone 0 clearance — and not the behavior of the whole envelope under radiant heat. We designed My Villa around that distinction: a reinforced concrete shell engineered with Transsolar's climate logic treats fire performance as a structural property, not a layered defense. When certifications evolve — and they will — a home conceived at the system level doesn't need to be retrofitted to keep up.
body_html:
The Insurance Institute for Business & Home Safety (IBHS) announced on April 14, 2026 that it has expanded its Wildfire Prepared Home program to ten additional states, extending a certification framework that originated as a California response to the state's insurance crisis. The move, reported by PR Newswire, signals that what began as a regional research standard is on track to become a de facto national benchmark for how carriers evaluate wildfire risk at the parcel level.

For California homeowners, the expansion is less a policy change and more a validation: the standard that local insurers already reference is becoming the language the rest of the country uses to talk about wildfire risk.

From California pilot to national framework

IBHS launched Wildfire Prepared Home in California in 2022 as a research-backed designation defining what a genuinely wildfire-hardened home looks like: a Class A non-combustible roof, ember-resistant vents, a five-foot non-combustible Zone 0, enclosed eaves, and — at the Plus tier — a non-combustible structural envelope. The program was designed as a technical standard, not a marketing label, and it was engineered to be auditable by insurers.

The IBHS designation requirements are the most stringent voluntary wildfire standard recognized by major carriers. In California, the designation intersects directly with the state's Safer from Wildfires framework administered by the California Department of Insurance, which obligates insurers to reflect verified mitigation in pricing and underwriting decisions.

10new states added to Wildfire Prepared
2certification tiers (Home and Home Plus)
1national standard emerging for insurers

The PR Newswire announcement confirms the program is moving beyond its West Coast origins. For national carriers that write in multiple states, a single certification framework reduces underwriting friction — and raises the floor for what qualifies as a well-mitigated home.

What the expansion signals for Los Angeles

The immediate Los Angeles implication is not regulatory — California's rules don't change when Colorado or Oregon adopt the same standard. The implication is market structural. When a certification becomes national, three things tend to follow: insurer product teams standardize their discount matrices around it, reinsurers begin modeling against it, and lenders start asking about it at origination. Each of those shifts concentrates value on homes that can demonstrate certification rather than just describe mitigation.

For high-value homes in Malibu, Beverly Hills, and the Westside canyons — where admitted-market capacity is already thin and FAIR Plan exposure is common — this matters in a specific way. A property that carries Wildfire Prepared Home Plus is no longer just "hardened." It is cataloged inside a framework that every carrier in the program now reads the same way. That legibility is the point.

The underwriting logic beneath the standard

What makes Wildfire Prepared commercially meaningful is its research base. IBHS built the standard from full-scale ember and radiant-heat testing at its South Carolina lab, not from committee consensus. The requirements exist because they are the variables that actually determine whether a home survives an ember storm. That empirical foundation is why carriers recognize it — and why its expansion tends to pull underwriting toward a more physical, less actuarial view of risk.

For homeowners commissioning new construction in California fire zones, the practical takeaway is that designing to the certification is easier and cheaper than retrofitting to it. Every element Wildfire Prepared requires — non-combustible cladding, enclosed eaves, compliant vents, defensible landscape — is simpler to specify on day one than to add later.

Looking forward

National expansion is a leading indicator. As Wildfire Prepared becomes shorthand in ten more states, California's luxury market should expect the standard to migrate further into lending, appraisal, and resale conversations. Homes designed around the certification — rather than toward it — will be the ones that stay legible to the insurance market as the framework continues to evolve.
excerpt: IBHS just extended its Wildfire Prepared Home certification — the technical standard behind California underwriting — into ten additional states. Here's why that changes the calculus for luxury homes in Los Angeles.
